name: Tamayori-hime
shortDescription: A divine woman in Japanese mythology, sister of Toyotama-hime, and mother of Emperor Jimmu's father, Ugayafukiaezu.
description: Tamayori-hime (玉依毘売命) is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, appearing prominently in the Kojiki. She is the younger sister of Toyotama-hime, the wife of Hoori (also known as Yamasachi-hiko). Tamayori-hime is best known as the mother of Ugayafukiaezu, who in turn is the father of Emperor Jimmu, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Her name translates to "spirit-attracting maiden", hinting at her role as a medium or a conduit for the divine. The Kojiki describes her miraculous conception of Ugayafukiaezu after being visited by a mysterious and handsome man, later revealed to be a dragon deity. This divine lineage underscores the sacred origins of the Japanese imperial family and highlights Tamayori-hime's pivotal role in connecting the human and divine realms. She represents purity, divine selection, and the continuation of the imperial line.
subtitle: Spirit-Attracting Maiden of Divine Lineage
role: Mother of a key ancestor in the imperial lineage

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Tamayori-hime's story is primarily found in the Kojiki, one of the oldest chronicles of Japanese history and mythology. She is the younger sister of Toyotama-hime, who married the human prince Hoori. After Toyotama-hime returned to the sea, Hoori was left with their child. Tamayori-hime raised the child, and later, a mysterious man began visiting her. This man was revealed to be a dragon deity (or, in some interpretations, the child's father, Hoori, in a transformed state). From this union, she conceived Ugayafukiaezu. This unusual conception is significant because it establishes a direct link between the imperial family and the dragon deities, powerful symbols of the sea and divine power. The narrative reinforces the divine mandate of the imperial line.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Tamayori-hime's significance lies in her role as the mother of Ugayafukiaezu, who is the father of Emperor Jimmu. By bearing Ugayafukiaezu, she directly contributes to the establishment of the Japanese imperial lineage. Her divine conception emphasizes the sacred nature of the imperial family's origins, imbuing them with divine authority and legitimacy. She is venerated as a divine ancestor and a symbol of purity and divine selection. Her story is a cornerstone of Japanese creation myths and reinforces the belief in the divine origins of the nation's rulers. She is also seen as a symbol of female power and agency, chosen by the gods to carry on the divine lineage.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Tamayori-hime's name, meaning "spirit-attracting maiden", suggests her role as a medium or conduit for divine spirits. She symbolizes the connection between the human and divine realms, acting as a bridge between the mortal world and the realm of the gods. Her story can be interpreted as a representation of the divine intervention in human affairs, particularly in the establishment of a ruling dynasty. The dragon deity who impregnates her symbolizes power, fertility, and the connection to the natural world. Her purity and chosen status highlight the importance of lineage and divine favor in Japanese mythology and culture. She embodies the ideal of a woman chosen by the gods for a sacred purpose.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tamayori-hime's legacy continues to resonate in Japanese culture through her role as a divine ancestor of the imperial family. Her story is often referenced in historical and mythological texts, and she is sometimes venerated in Shinto shrines. While not as widely worshipped as some other deities, her significance as a foundational figure in the imperial lineage ensures her continued recognition. In modern times, she is often portrayed in art, literature, and popular culture as a symbol of female strength, divine connection, and the sacred origins of Japan. Her story continues to fascinate and inspire, serving as a reminder of the rich mythological heritage of Japan.

sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro
sources.description: The Kojiki is the oldest extant chronicle of Japan, detailing myths, legends, and the imperial lineage. It is the primary source for Tamayori-hime's story.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation and annotation of the Kojiki, providing valuable insights into the mythology and cultural context of Tamayori-hime's story.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: The dragon deity is her consort and the source of her divine pregnancy, symbolizing power and fertility.

associations.name: Purity
associations.significance: Her purity is essential to her role as a vessel for divine conception.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Connected to the dragon deity and the sea, symbolizing life and creation.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Virgin Mary
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both figures experience divine conceptions and bear children of significant religious importance.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Danaë
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are impregnated by a deity (Zeus in Danaë's case) and give birth to a hero (Perseus).

companions.name: Toyotama-hime
companions.description: Elder sister of Tamayori-hime and wife of Hoori.

companions.name: Ugayafukiaezu
companions.description: Son of Tamayori-hime and father of Emperor Jimmu.

companions.name: Hoori
companions.description: Husband of Toyotama-hime, potentially the dragon deity who impregnated Tamayori-hime.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestor veneration
worshipPractices.description: As a divine ancestor of the imperial family, she is indirectly venerated through practices honoring the imperial line.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tamayori-hime is a symbol of divine lineage and female strength in modern Japanese culture, often appearing in historical dramas and literature.
